tldr: OpenSearch is the Apache 2.0 fork of Elasticsearch and Kibana 7.10.2 — the last open-source release before Elastic relicensed under SSPL / Elastic License 2.0 in 2021. The fork is led by AWS and now hosted by the Linux Foundation. Migration makes sense when you want to escape Elastic License restrictions (no managed-service resale, no telemetry / billing carve-outs), get the security plugin and alerting for free (these were paid X-Pack tiers), or align with an AWS / open-source-only stack. Mechanically the migration is a snapshot-restore from a 7.10-era cluster; the further your source ES has drifted past 7.10.2, the more reindexing and feature rework you sign up for.
why_switch: License: OpenSearch is Apache 2.0; Elasticsearch 7.11+ is SSPL / Elastic License 2.0, which restricts offering it as a managed service and adds field-of-use limits.

Free Security plugin: TLS, RBAC, role mappings, audit logging, SAML / OIDC, document-level security and field-level security ship in OpenSearch out of the box — these were paid X-Pack Platinum/Enterprise tiers in Elasticsearch.
Free Alerting and Anomaly Detection: equivalent of Watcher and basic ML jobs, included by default rather than gated behind a commercial subscription.
AWS-managed option: Amazon OpenSearch Service is a first-class managed offering, which Elastic's license has historically blocked third-party clouds from offering for newer ES versions.
Inclusive language defaults: cluster_manager replaces master node terminology; this is a non-functional but real cultural / compliance reason cited by some teams.
Linux Foundation governance: vendor-neutral foundation steers the project rather than a single commercial company, reducing rug-pull risk for downstream users.
Cost predictability: no per-node X-Pack subscription tiers — you pay only for the infrastructure (or AWS managed pricing) without the open-core paywall.
what_to_export: Index data — primary documents in each index, ideally captured via snapshot rather than reindex.
Index mappings, settings and aliases — stored inside the snapshot; review for any 8.x-only field types if your source is post-fork.
Index templates and component templates — composable templates introduced in 7.x are supported by OpenSearch.
Snapshot repository definitions — S3, filesystem, Azure or GCS repository configs (the repository itself is recreated on the target).
Kibana saved objects — dashboards, visualizations, index patterns, lenses, canvas workpads — exported as NDJSON via Kibana's Saved Objects API.
Watcher definitions — exported via the _watcher API; will need to be rebuilt as OpenSearch Alerting monitors (no automatic converter).
ILM policies — exported via _ilm/policy; will need to be rewritten as ISM policies (similar concepts, different schema).
Ingest pipelines — exported via _ingest/pipeline; processor set is largely compatible since both descend from 7.10.
Transforms and rollup jobs — exported via _transform / _rollup APIs; OpenSearch has equivalent features but config schemas differ.

Role mappings, roles, users and API keys — Elastic Security and OpenSearch Security store these differently; expect to redefine in the OpenSearch Security plugin's internal_users / roles_mapping YAML or API.
ML / Anomaly Detection jobs — Elastic ML is paid and proprietary; OpenSearch Anomaly Detection is a separate plugin with its own job format, so jobs are recreated, not imported.
Cluster settings and snapshot lifecycle (SLM) policies — review and reapply manually; SLM in ES maps to Snapshot Management (SM) in OpenSearch.
export_method: Recommended path: take a snapshot from your current Elasticsearch cluster to a shared repository (S3, filesystem, Azure, or GCS) using PUT _snapshot/<repo>/<snapshot_name>?wait_for_completion=true. The cleanest case is when your source cluster is on Elasticsearch 7.10.x — that snapshot can be restored directly into a current OpenSearch cluster because OpenSearch was forked from 7.10.2. If your source has drifted to 7.11+ or 8.x, you typically need to either (a) downgrade-export by reindexing into a 7.10-compatible index format first, or (b) use the OpenSearch Migration Assistant tooling to bridge versions. Independently export Kibana saved objects via Saved Objects API → NDJSON, and dump Watcher / ILM / role definitions via their respective REST APIs to JSON files held in version control.
import_method: On the OpenSearch side: register the same snapshot repository (PUT _snapshot/<repo>), then restore selected indices via POST _snapshot/<repo>/<snapshot_name>/_restore with an indices filter and rename pattern if needed. Bring up OpenSearch Dashboards and import the NDJSON saved-objects bundle (Stack Management → Saved Objects → Import); some Kibana-only object types may be skipped or need manual recreation. Recreate Watcher logic as monitors in the OpenSearch Alerting plugin (per-bucket, per-document, or cluster-metric monitors). Translate ILM policies into ISM policies (states + transitions + actions). Configure security via the OpenSearch Security plugin's YAML config (roles.yml, roles_mapping.yml, internal_users.yml) or its REST API. Smoke-test query DSL, then run dual-write or shadow-read for a period before cutting clients over.

concept_mapping.notes: Forked from Kibana 7.10.2; saved-object NDJSON import works for dashboards, visualizations, index patterns. Lens and Canvas may need rebuilding.
TLS, RBAC, SAML, OIDC, document- and field-level security — free in OpenSearch. Roles and mappings are redefined, not imported as-is.
Concepts overlap (triggers, conditions, actions) but config schema differs — manual rewrite per watch.
States + transitions + actions model. No automatic policy converter; rewrite each phase (hot/warm/cold/delete) as ISM states.
Cron-based snapshot scheduling; recreate policies on the target.
Different algorithms and job schema. Treat as a rebuild, not a migration.
Renamed terminology; old master_* settings are aliased for back-compat but deprecated.
RHLC is deprecated in ES 7.15+; OpenSearch ships its own opensearch-java and a fork of the high-level REST client. Some apps can stay on a 7.10-era client during the cutover.

concept_mapping.to: OpenSearch-compatible Beats fork or Data Prepper
concept_mapping.notes: Recent Beats versions check ES product headers and refuse to talk to non-Elastic clusters; use OpenSearch's compatibility flag, the OpenSearch Beats fork, or migrate ingestion to Data Prepper / Fluent Bit.
Same conceptual feature; recreate jobs via _transform API.
Different stack; rebuild monitoring dashboards rather than importing.
Both exist in OpenSearch but config and naming differ; CCR is a separate plugin.

effort: 2-8 weeks for a single-team production cluster. Lower end (around 2 weeks) if you are already on Elasticsearch 7.10.x with vanilla open-source features (no Watcher, no ILM, no X-Pack Security). Upper end (6-8+ weeks) if you are on ES 8.x, depend heavily on Watcher / ILM / ML jobs, or run multi-tenant Kibana spaces with custom RBAC — those areas all require manual rebuild, not snapshot restore.
gotchas: The clean snapshot-restore path is Elasticsearch 7.10.x → OpenSearch. Versions 7.11 and later changed snapshot/index formats; restoring those into OpenSearch may require reindexing or the Migration Assistant tooling rather than a direct restore.

Anything that lived under X-Pack proprietary features — Lens visualizations, Canvas, Graph, Elastic ML, Fleet/Agent, Enterprise Search, APM in its current form — does not have a 1:1 OpenSearch equivalent. Plan to drop or rebuild these.

Beats and Logstash 7.14+ added an Elasticsearch product-version handshake that rejects non-Elastic clusters by default. You either run a slightly older shipper, set the documented compatibility/override flag, or move ingestion to Data Prepper or Fluent Bit.

The Java REST High Level Client was deprecated by Elastic in 7.15 and removed in 8.x. Application code that still uses it will need to migrate to opensearch-java (or the OpenSearch RHLC fork) before or during cutover.

Query DSL has diverged after the 7.10 fork point. Most common queries still parse identically, but newer ES features (e.g. some search-after enhancements, vector / kNN syntax, ES|QL, runtime fields) either don't exist in OpenSearch or use a different syntax (OpenSearch has its own kNN plugin and a separate PPL language).
Watcher and ILM do not auto-convert. Both have direct conceptual replacements (Alerting and ISM) but the JSON schemas are different — budget rewrite time per watch and per policy.

Security config is not portable. Elastic's native realm / role definitions must be redefined in the OpenSearch Security plugin's YAML or via its API; SAML / OIDC integrations need to be reconfigured against the new metadata endpoints.

ML licensing differs: Elastic ML is a paid feature and its models / jobs are not exported in a portable way. OpenSearch Anomaly Detection is free but uses different detectors — treat the ML layer as a green-field rebuild and validate detection quality before retiring the old jobs.

email.subject: Migrating from Elasticsearch to OpenSearch — plan and timeline
email.body:
Hi team,

Proposing we move our Elasticsearch cluster to OpenSearch. Quick context: OpenSearch is the Apache 2.0 fork of Elasticsearch / Kibana 7.10.2 — created after Elastic moved to SSPL / Elastic License 2.0 in 2021. It's now governed by the Linux Foundation with AWS as a primary contributor.

Why this is worth doing now:
  - License: Apache 2.0 vs SSPL / ELv2 — no field-of-use restrictions, no managed-service carve-outs.
  - Cost: TLS, RBAC, SAML / OIDC, document- and field-level security, alerting, and anomaly detection all ship for free. These were paid X-Pack tiers for us.
  - Optionality: Amazon OpenSearch Service is a first-class managed target if we ever want to offload ops.

Migration plan (rough 2-8 week range depending on what we keep):
  1. Audit current usage — confirm which X-Pack features (Watcher, ILM, Lens, ML) we actually rely on. Anything proprietary needs a rebuild plan.
  2. Pin to ES 7.10.x or use the OpenSearch Migration Assistant — the snapshot-restore path is cleanest from 7.10. If we are on 7.11+ or 8.x, expect reindexing.
  3. Snapshot to S3 from the current cluster, restore into a fresh OpenSearch cluster, validate index counts and document totals.
  4. Rebuild: Watcher → Alerting monitors, ILM → ISM policies, Kibana saved objects → import NDJSON into OpenSearch Dashboards, Beats / Logstash either pinned to compatible versions or replaced by Data Prepper.
  5. Reconfigure security in the OpenSearch Security plugin (roles, role mappings, SAML / OIDC).
  6. Dual-write or shadow-read window, then cut clients over and decommission ES.

Main risks to flag up-front:
  - Anything dependent on Elastic ML, Lens, Canvas, Fleet/APM-in-its-current-form, or the Java REST High Level Client needs explicit migration work.
  - Beats / Logstash 7.14+ have a product-version check that rejects non-Elastic clusters by default; we will need the compatibility flag or a different shipper.

Happy to scope a small spike against a copy of the cluster before we commit to a date.

Thanks
